contributor: george iv, king of great britain
description: 1 print on laid paper : aquatint and etching  ; 31.1 x 37.2 cm (plate), 32 x 38.5 cm (sheet) | Print shows two fashionably dressed women walking on a country road, one is carrying a purse on her left hand; they ignore a "blind" beggar with a small dog on a leash and holding a cup and stick in right hand. As they pass, he steps on the dress of the women with the purse, causing her dress to tear and exposing her bare buttocks. A satire on the miserliness of the new upper class, as well as the contemporary clothing fashions of the merveilleuse.

related_items.title: Ah! s'il y voyoit!

contributor: george iv, king of great britain
description: 1 print : engraving with etching. | Print shows four bare-bottomed men, the ministers of finances, sitting around a table restoring their good health on the produce of other nations; as they consume the "Eau de la Plata" and "Eau du Sénégal," the "Terre du Potosi en Sorbet" and the "Terre du Perou" they excrete coins through holes in their chairs. On the left, another man urinates coins into a funnel in a box. This print may have counterrevolutionary overtones, suggesting that the Directory is deluding the people into believing that by continuing war and conquering other lands France will be able to restore its own financial health. The caption may suggest that it is as impossible to excrete wealth as it is to restore wealth through imperialist ventures.

related_items.title: La chose impossible, ou la commission des finances telle quil la faudroit pour les bien restaurer

description: 1 print : etching and roulette. | Print shows Pope Pius VI standing on one side of a river with two ecclesiastical aids; he is so alarmed at the arrival of the French Army on the opposite side of the river next to a castle flying the Papal flag, that he ejaculates on a mooring (perhaps seen here as an anchorage of moral and spiritual resources), thus begetting Pius VII.

description: 1 print : etching and engraving. | Print shows on the left, a female figure representing the Republic of France drawing a slip of paper from an urn labeled "Directoire de la République Francaise" with Etienne-François-Louis-Honoré Le Tourneur's name on it, she turns and indicates that Le Tourneur must relinquish his position as one of the five leaders of the Directory, his cape and sword lie on the floor as he hastens to depart on the right; the remaining four leaders dance together in the center background.
